Balwadi (early childhood care center) by Rashtra Sewa Bharti  marked a new chapter in community well-being. Before its establishment, young children spent their days unattended, missing out on essential learning and nutrition. Need for more space, learning materials, and infrastructure.  Encouraging regular participation can sometimes be difficult due to family responsibilities and social barriers.","success_story":"A story of a young girl in the kendra. She used to sit at the edge. Not just physically — though that too. At every session, she chose the seat furthest from the centre of the room, closest to the door, as though she needed to know that leaving was always an option. When the group discussed, she looked at her hands. When her name was called, something in her face closed like a shutter. She was present, but only just — a girl who had learned, somewhere between childhood and adolescence, that her voice was not something the world particularly needed to hear. This is not an uncommon story for a girl growing up in the kind of community this Kishori Vikas Kendra serves. By the time she arrived at the Kendra, she had already absorbed years of a very specific education — the education that tells a girl that smallness is safety, that silence is the wisest policy, that the best thing she can do is take up as little space as possible. The Kishori Vikas Kendra had a different curriculum. It did not begin with grand interventions. It began with the ordinary rhythm of showing up — the group discussions where every voice was not just permitted but expected, the cultural activities where a girl who had never performed in front of anyone was gently placed in front of a small, forgiving audience of her peers, the guidance sessions where the women who ran the Kendra spoke frankly about what a girl's life could look like if she chose to build it deliberately rather than simply receive whatever it handed her. None of this was dramatic. All of it was consistent. She kept coming back. The first time she spoke in a group discussion — a real contribution, not just a murmured agreement — she surprised herself more than anyone else. Her voice, when she finally used it with intention, turned out to be steadier than she had expected. The room did not fall apart. The other girls did not laugh. The woman leading the session looked at her with the particular expression of someone who had been waiting for this moment and was genuinely glad it had arrived. She came back the next week and sat a little less close to the door. The transformation that followed was not a sudden blossoming — those happen in stories, not in lives. What happened was slower and more solid than that. She became someone who prepared for discussions rather than dreading them. Someone who arrived early. Someone who noticed, with the particular attention of a person who has recently discovered her own capability, when another girl at the edge of the room was sitting the way she used to sit — and who went and sat beside her, not to drag her to the centre, but simply to make the edge feel less lonely. That instinct — to turn around and reach for the girl behind her — is the real measure of what this Kendra produced. This is how the Kishori Vikas Kendra has grown. Not through campaigns or announcements, but through the oldest and most reliable form of outreach there is , one girl telling another that there is a place where she will be listened to, where her confidence is not a problem to be managed but a capacity to be built, where the women who run the sessions have made it their work to ensure that no girl leaves the way she arrived. She is not extraordinary in the way that word is usually meant. She did not overcome something catastrophic. She did not achieve something the world will notice. What she did was quieter and, in its own way, more radical: she decided that her voice mattered, in a world that had spent years suggesting otherwise. And then she used it — first for herself, then for every girl in her neighbourhood who needed someone to show them that it was possible. The Kishori Vikas Kendra gave her the conditions to make that decision.","photo_availability":"Yes","video_status":"Yes","district_name":"Central","state_name":"Delhi (दिल्ली)","village_name":"Badar Starting with just a few women, Rajni initiated a structured 6-month silai (tailoring) course, teaching techniques from basic stitching to complex garment design. But more than that, she fostered an environment of trust, dignity, and hope. बल्कि रोगियों को बिना साइड इफेक्ट के दीर्घकालिक राहत भी प्रदान करती है।","impact":"इस सेवा के चलते दिल्ली में होम्योपैथिक ओपीडीज़ की संख्या में उल्लेखनीय वृद्धि हुई है, जिससे अधिक से अधिक लोगों को सुलभ चिकित्सा सुविधा प्राप्त हो रही है। निःशुल्क और सुरक्षित उपचार के कारण रोगियों की संतुष्टि और होम्योपैथी के प्रति विश्वास लगातार बढ़ रहा है। पहले जो लोग इसे एक धीमी चिकित्सा पद्धति मानते थे, अब वे इसकी प्रभावशीलता और पारंपरिक उपचार के लाभों को अनुभव कर रहे हैं। इस विश्वास ने न केवल मरीजों की संख्या में वृद्धि की है, बल्कि स्थानीय समुदाय को भी आयुर्वेद और होम्योपैथी जैसे वैकल्पिक चिकित्सा की ओर आकर्षित किया है।","achievements":"होम्योपैथिक ओपीडी की सेवाओं के माध्यम से सैकड़ों कुष्ठ रोगियों को रोगमुक्त किया गया, जिससे उन्हें समाज में पुनः सम्मानजनक जीवन जीने का अवसर मिला। यह केंद्र न केवल कुष्ठ रोग तक सीमित रहा, बल्कि जनरल ओपीडी के माध्यम से क्षेत्र के हर प्रकार के रोगी—जैसे त्वचा रोग, श्वसन समस्याएं, पाचन विकार, व महिलाओं और बच्चों से संबंधित समस्याओं—को भी लाभ पहुंचा रहा है। रोगियों की नियमित जांच, सही परामर्श और निःशुल्क दवाओं के माध्यम से स्वास्थ्य में निरंतर सुधार हुआ है। यह उपलब्धि क्षेत्र में एक विश्वासपूर्ण और प्रभावी चिकित्सा विकल्प के रूप में इस केंद्र की पहचान को और भी मजबूत करती है।","challenges":"हालांकि होम्योपैथिक ओपीडी क्षेत्र में उल्लेखनीय कार्य कर रही है, लेकिन इसके संचालन में कुछ महत्वपूर्ण चुनौतियाँ भी सामने आती हैं। पर्याप्त प्रशिक्षित और अनुभवी चिकित्सकों की कमी के कारण कभी-कभी रोगियों को समय पर परामर्श नहीं मिल पाता, जिससे सेवाओं की निरंतरता प्रभावित होती है। इसके अतिरिक्त, कई जटिल रोगों के इलाज में आधुनिक चिकित्सा प्रणाली के साथ समन्वय की आवश्यकता महसूस होती है, ताकि गंभीर मरीजों को समय रहते उचित उपचार मिल सके। इन चुनौतियों के बावजूद, सेवा केंद्र अपने संसाधनों के माध्यम से लगातार सुधार की दिशा में प्रयासरत है।","success_story":"दिल्ली के The environment at the center was unlike anything she had seen—structured, values-based, and full of care. Each morning began with prarthana and a patriotic song, reminding her that she belonged to something bigger than her struggles. At the Cutting and Tailoring Center run by Seva Bharti since 2010, women from underprivileged communities are finding not just a new skill—but a new sense of self.  These women arrive with little—some with no prior training, some carrying the weight of financial hardship, and many with shattered confidence. But once inside the single-room center, something changes. Here, they are taught the fundamentals of stitching, fabric handling, and basic garment construction. More importantly, they are encouraged to believe that their hands can shape their future.  Over weeks of training, hesitancy gives way to precision. With each cut of cloth and carefully crafted seam, a deeper transformation begins. Women begin to stitch not only fabric, but also their own path to dignity and self-reliance. Many begin earning by taking stitching orders from neighbors and local markets. Their small incomes grow into meaningful support for their families.  Mothers are now able to pay for their children's education. Daughters who once watched from the sidelines now dream of starting their own workspaces. The impact ripples beyond income—it reshapes their identity. These are women who were once silenced by circumstance, now standing strong with skills and confidence.  This effort directly contributes to SDG 5 (Gender Equality) and SDG 8 (Decent Work and Economic Growth), creating livelihood opportunities where there were none, and ensuring no woman is left behind due to her economic or social background. A place where self-worth is sewn with every stitch, where the threads of tradition are used to create new possibilities, and where each woman becomes the designer of her own destiny. में प्रोत्साहित करना है। इसके माध्यम से सभी वर्गों के लोगों को, विशेष रूप से गरीब और वंचित समुदायों को, सुलभ, सुरक्षित और किफायती उपचार उपलब्ध कराया जा रहा है। यह पहल न केवल आर्थिक बोझ को कम करती है, बल्कि रोगियों को बिना साइड इफेक्ट के दीर्घकालिक राहत भी प्रदान करती है।","impact":"इस सेवा के चलते दिल्ली में होम्योपैथिक ओपीडीज़ की संख्या में उल्लेखनीय वृद्धि हुई है, जिससे अधिक से अधिक लोगों को सुलभ चिकित्सा सुविधा प्राप्त हो रही है। निःशुल्क और सुरक्षित उपचार के कारण रोगियों की संतुष्टि और होम्योपैथी के प्रति विश्वास लगातार बढ़ रहा है। पहले जो लोग इसे एक धीमी चिकित्सा पद्धति मानते थे, अब वे इसकी प्रभावशीलता और पारंपरिक उपचार के लाभों को अनुभव कर रहे हैं। इस विश्वास ने न केवल मरीजों की संख्या में वृद्धि की है, बल्कि स्थानीय समुदाय को भी आयुर्वेद और होम्योपैथी जैसे वैकल्पिक चिकित्सा की ओर Her father was a daily wage laborer, and school had been a distant dream. Komal barely knew how to thread a needle, but she was determined to learn. The centre not only taught her stitching and cutting but also built her confidence through daily discipline and patriotic prayer.  After completing her six-month training, Komal began stitching women’s wear and school uniforms from home. Gradually, her work gained recognition. With her earnings, she bought her own sewing machine and contributed to household expenses. Today, she earns ₹4,000–₹6,000 per month, and more importantly, is regarded with respect in her basti. Bhajan Mandali became that safe and nurturing space that reinforces identity, devotion, and togetherness through the power of collective prayer.","impact":"Over the years, the Bhajan Mandali has created a strong sense of community, purpose, and peace among the women of Priyanka Camp. It has offered them emotional support, moral grounding, and cultural continuity. Many women now play leadership roles in religious events and serve as spiritual anchors in their families and neighborhoods. The mandali has elevated the role of women from passive homemakers to active custodians of dharma and social harmony.","achievements":"The Mandali has successfully united more than 50 women in a sustained spiritual movement rooted in Indian traditions. It has cultivated a culture of regular devotion, mutual care, and cultural participation among urban poor women—an achievement both rare and remarkable in such socio-economic settings. Women now lead kirtans, organize religious functions, and independently carry forward the legacy of faith and service. This silent cultural revolution has turned the service basti into a spiritually awakened and socially cohesive space.","challenges":"The biggest hurdle remains financial sustainability. With no salaried staff or external institutional support, all efforts depend on limited volunteer strength and self-motivation. Access to devotional materials, instruments like dholak/harmonium, and suitable space for larger gatherings during festivals is often lacking. Despite ownership of the building, infrastructural upgrades and provision of basic amenities like fans and sound systems remain unfulfilled. Yet, the spirit of the women sustains the mandali with steadfast devotion.","success_story":"Since its inception in 1998, the Bhajan Mandali at Priyanka Camp has transformed into a powerful spiritual platform where voices of devotion blend with stories of inner awakening. One such story is of Meena didi, who joined the mandali during a period of deep personal grief. Her husband had passed away, and she had isolated herself emotionally. It was a neighbor who gently urged her to come to a bhajan session. Reluctantly, she agreed.  At first, she sat in silence. But as weeks passed, the collective singing, warm presence of other women, and the vibrations of devotion began to heal her. She started singing along, leading bhajans, and eventually began helping coordinate festival events. Today, Meena didi is one of the central figures in the mandali—radiating positivity and strength. Her journey inspired many others to rise beyond sorrow and hardship.  This mandali has proven that faith, when nurtured collectively, becomes a force of transformation. It is not just a place for worship—it is a sanctuary of healing, empowerment, and silent leadership by women who may not hold titles but lead with their hearts, voices, and और परामर्श नहीं मिल पाता। इसके अतिरिक्त, आधुनिक चिकित्सा प्रणाली के साथ समन्वय की कमी भी एक बड़ी बाधा है, जिससे गंभीर या आपातकालीन मामलों में मरीजों को उचित रेफरल या बहु-विध चिकित्सा सहयोग प्राप्त माइग्रेन और अनिद्रा जैसी समस्याओं से परेशान थीं। सिरदर्द इतना तीव्र होता था कि वह कई बार दिनभर कुछ भी नहीं कर पाती थीं। नींद की कमी और थकान ने उनकी दिनचर्या को तोड़ दिया था, जिससे उनके परिवार और मानसिक स्थिति पर भी प्रभाव पड़ने लगा था।  उन्होंने वर्षों तक एलोपैथिक इलाज करवाया, कई डॉक्टरों से परामर्श लिया, महंगी दवाइयाँ लीं, लेकिन राहत केवल अस्थायी रही। दवाओं के दुष्प्रभाव और मानसिक थकावट ने उन्हें हताश कर दिया था। जीवन धीरे-धीरे बोझ बनता जा रहा था।  तभी एक दिन पड़ोस की एक महिला ने उन्हें सेवा भारती द्वारा संचालित स्थानीय होम्योपैथिक ओपीडी के बारे में बताया। पहले तो सीमा को संदेह हुआ, ","achievements":"Financial independence awareness and a platform for dialogue.","challenges":"Absence of a permanent dedicated space ","success_story":"In Molarband, the women came quietly at first, one by one, some hesitant, some simply curious. They came from different lanes, different circumstances. What they had in common was not obvious until they sat together and the bhajan began. The Bhajan Mandali did not set out to solve anything. It set out to gather. But gathering, it turned out, solved more than anyone had expected. Women who had lived as neighbours for years without occasion to speak found themselves, week after week, sitting in the same room, learning the same words, sharing the same silences between songs. And in those silences, something grew — the particular ease that comes from having been present with someone repeatedly, without agenda, without transaction. Today, those women call this mandali their own. They come for the bhajan. They stay for each other. When someone is unwell, the others know. When someone's family is struggling, the circle closes around them. This is what this centre has built, sung together, week after week — it turns neighbours into community, and community into family. In a corner of Delhi that the city moves past without noticing, this mandali has made something that does not appear on any map but is more real than most things that No one knew his name, his family, or the story behind his arrival. He had been found abandoned and wrapped in a thin cloth, crying helplessly. The caregivers gently lifted him in their arms and promised silently that he would never feel alone again.  They named him Aarav.  In the beginning he would often wake up at night crying, as if searching for someone he could not remember. The caregivers held him close, sang lullabies, and slowly became the family he had lost. Chronic illness, when coupled with economic vulnerability, can completely erode not just health, but also dignity and hope. But for Kalpana and Archana, the Seva Bharti Diagnostic and Dialysis Centre became more than a medical facility—it became their lifeline.  Kalpana, a young widow from Sawan Park, lost her husband at just 29. The emotional blow quickly manifested physically—headaches, swelling, and dangerously high blood pressure. A visit to Ganga Ram Hospital revealed the cruel truth: complete kidney failure. Every dialysis session cost ₹3000–₹3500—far beyond what she could afford. Between choosing food for her daughter or treatment for herself, she stood on the edge of collapse. That is when Seva Bharti stepped in, offering completely free dialysis from April 2020 onward. Kalpana’s health stabilized, and with it, her courage returned. She now raises her daughter with renewed hope, knowing that inclusive healthcare access has given her a second chance at life.  Archana’s journey, equally moving, began with a fever that masked a deeper danger—her kidneys were shrinking. Just 24 at the time, she was pursuing her M.Com and working, but soon had to leave both due to deteriorating health. Her family slipped into debt trying to fund treatment. But Archana chose not to surrender. She approached Seva Bharti and was not only welcomed as a patient but later offered a receptionist job, and eventually promoted as an assistant accountant—a testament to how the centre prioritizes economic empowerment and upskilling even during adversity.  Both stories are emblematic of the prakalp’s broader mission—meeting Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) 3 (Good Health and Well-being), 5 (Gender Equality), 8 (Decent Work and Economic Growth), and 10 (Reduced Inequalities).  This prakalp breaks the cycle of capability deprivation and ushers in intergenerational social mobility. It restores social protection where none existed, champions redistributive justice, and builds a learning and healing ecosystem where health is not a privilege, but a right. Government facilities are often distant, overcrowded, and inconsistently functional. A persistent discomfort he could not name. He had learned, over years of working in Delhi, that the body's warnings were something you managed privately, without involving the healthcare system, because the healthcare system involved costs he could not manage. That there was a place where he could seek help without the fear of financial consequences. That sentence, simple and direct, is the most precise description of what this centre does. In 2023, she was enrolled in the Shiksha Kendra run by Seva Bharti Delhi.  Initially shy and irregular, Zoya gradually started participating in patriotic song sessions and group reading classes. The personal attention from the teacher, regular moral lessons, and peer encouragement built her confidence. Within a few months, she could read her textbooks fluently and even helped her younger brother learn alphabets. Her school teachers noticed the improvement, and Zoya began scoring better in exams.  Today, Zoya is seen proudly reciting Hindi poems and participating in national festival programs at the Kendra. Her story is a reflection of social inclusion, access to opportunity, and human capital development enabled by grassroots educational efforts. The Kendra has become a symbol of inclusive growth in a locality otherwise forgotten by formal systems. Instances of mutual cooperation — neighbours helping during illness, collective celebration of festivals, women coordinating community welfare — have grown visibly in the lanes where Bhajan Mandali members reside. The prakalp serves women across age groups, with no restriction on socio-economic background, reflecting the non-discriminatory spirit ","achievements":"Social Harmony.","challenges":"• Absence of a dedicated permanent space,  sessions depend on community goodwill, borrowed temple premises, or rented halls, creating continuity challenges  •Limited availability of trained instrument instructors willing to commit to voluntary teaching on a sustained basis","success_story":"In 2004, in a lane in Tagore Garden that looked like a hundred other lanes in West Delhi, something very quiet began. A few women gathered — not in a hall, not with an announcement, but simply because someone had thought it was time to bring the dholak back.  The neighbourhood had changed over the years. Families had come from everywhere — from the dusty towns of eastern UP, from the villages of Rajasthan, from the small cities of Bihar — each carrying their own forms of devotion. The Ramlila they had grown up watching. The mangal geet sung at weddings. The names of God whispered at dawn before anyone else in the house was awake. But urban life had a way of pushing all of that inward. There was no courtyard to sing in. No evening gathering. No one to teach the manjira to a daughter who had grown up here, in this city, with no memory of what her grandmother's hands had known.  The Bhajan Mandali, established under Sewa Bharati in 2004, did not promise anything grand. It offered a place to gather, a teacher willing to show how the harmonium breathes, and the simple companionship of women who wanted to remember something they felt they were forgetting. That was enough.  Twenty-one years later, the women who come here do not come because someone told them to. They come because the Bhajan Mandali has become a part of their week the way the kitchen and the temple are a part of their day. Sisters who had never touched an instrument now lead the sankeertan at Navratri. Women who migrated to Delhi not speaking a word of Sanskrit can now carry the full text of the Hanuman Chalisa without pausing. The dholak that felt foreign in the first year has become the rhythm that the children in the lane recognise — they know when practice has begun, they know when a festival is near.  This is what twenty-one years of sewa looks like — not a monument, but a sound. Not a statistic, but a habit. The devotional life that these women were in danger of losing to the noise and pace of the city has not been preserved in a museum. It has been kept alive in the way all living things are kept alive — by use, by practice, by being passed from one pair of hands to another.  And perhaps this is the deeper truth that the Bhajan Mandali of Tagore Garden has quietly demonstrated over two decades: that sewa does not always arrive as relief or rescue. Started with the intention of sewa bhaav and to instill the values of Sanatan Dharma in the hearts of people—especially the younger generation—the Prakalp has blossomed into a thriving spiritual movement.  Every day, children enthusiastically gather to recite the Hanuman Chalisa, showcasing not just interest but deep devotion. This has created a beautiful habit among them of staying connected with their roots. Women from the nearby areas also participate actively in bhajan and kirtan sessions, filling the temple premises with divine energy and positivity.  One of the most heartwarming aspects of the Prakalp is the bhandara events. Whenever a bhandara is organized, people from nearby areas come forward voluntarily to help—offering physical support, bringing supplies, or even contributing financially. If anything is missing, they take the initiative to ask and fulfill the requirement. This spirit of togetherness and service has become a hallmark of the Prakalp.  Festival celebrations, particularly Hanuman Jayanti, have witnessed overwhelming participation and devotion. The Prakalp has not only strengthened religious sentiments but also built a close-knit, spiritually awakened community. What began as a small devotional effort has now become a source of inspiration and unity, deeply rooted in tradition and service. विस्तार बाधित होता है। फिर भी केंद्र सेवा भावना से कार्य करता है और सीमित संसाधनों में अधिकतम लाभ देने का प्रयास करता है।","success_story":"दिल्ली का लाजपत नगर क्षेत्र बाहर से जितना चमकता और व्यवस्थित दिखता है, उसके भीतर उतनी ही गहराई से प्रवासी श्रमिकों की कठिन ज़िंदगी, बेरोज़गारी, और सामाजिक असमानता जमी हुई थी। विशेषकर उन परिवारों के लिए जो रोज़ाना की मजदूरी से अपना जीवन चलाते थे, यह क्षेत्र विकास का नहीं बल्कि संघर्ष का प्रतीक था।  इन्हीं परिस्थितियों को समझते हुए राष्ट्रीय सेवा भारती (RSB) ने लाजपत नगर में एक समर्पित सेवा केंद्र की स्थापना की — एक ऐसा केंद्र जिसका उद्देश्य केवल मदद करना नहीं, बल्कि लोगों को आत्मनिर्भर बनाना और उनके जीवन की दिशा बदलना था।  इस केंद्र में शुरू हुए शैक्षणिक, स्वास्थ्य और कौशल विकास कार्यक्रमों ने कई घरों की तस्वीर बदल दी। बच्चों के लिए निःशुल्क ट्यूशन, महिलाओं के लिए सिलाई-कढ़ाई प्रशिक्षण, और युवाओं के लिए कंप्यूटर कोर्स व करियर काउंसलिंग ने उनमें नई ऊर्जा और उद्देश्य भर दिए।  जहाँ पहले बच्चों की पढ़ाई छूटने की कगार पर थी, वहीं अब वे स्कूल में अच्छा प्रदर्शन करने लगे। महिलाएं, जो कभी घर के बाहर काम करने से डरती थीं, अब घर बैठे अपनी कला से आय अर्जित कर रही हैं। युवाओं को कंपनियों में इंटर्नशिप और नौकरियों के अवसर मिलने लगे हैं।  स्वास्थ्य सेवा के क्षेत्र में भी इस केंद्र ने अमूल्य योगदान दिया। फ्री मेडिकल चेकअप, नेत्र जांच शिविर, और फिजियोथेरेपी सेवाओं से सैकड़ों लोगों को लाभ मिला। इलाज की चिंता अब उनके लिए बीते दिनों की बात बन गई है।  इन सब सेवाओं ने न केवल स्वास्थ्य, शिक्षा और रोजगार को लोगों की ज़िंदगी से जोड़ा, बल्कि उनमें आत्मविश्वास और आत्मसम्मान की भावना भी जगाई। सबसे महत्वपूर्ण बात — लोगों ने महसूस किया कि वे अपनी ज़िंदगी खुद बदल सकते हैं।  आज RSB का यह केंद्र सिर्फ एक सेवा स्थान नहीं, बल्कि एक नई सोच, एक नई उम्मीद और एक सशक्त समाज के निर्माण की आधारशिला बन चुका है। लाजपत नगर के कई परिवार अब इसे अपना दूसरा घर मानते हैं — एक These sessions also highlighted the spiritual and cultural significance of Gosewa, linking practical knowledge with traditional values. What began as a humble gesture of compassion soon blossomed into a spiritually uplifting movement.  Week by week, more women joined—bringing with them rotis in their hands and devotion in their hearts. Their visits became more than acts of service; they turned into sacred rituals. The Gaushala transformed into a space of collective care, while the nearby temple resonated with the powerful recitations of the Sundarkand, offered not merely for personal blessings but for the welfare of all beings.  This growing circle of women inspired a quiet wave of spiritual connection and community engagement—instilling values of Gau Sewa, devotion, and shared responsibility. vehicles.","reasons_for_opening_the_prakalp":"The initiative emerged out of an urgent social need and organizational resolve to serve differently-abled children residing in East Delhi’s urban clusters. These children, especially from economically weaker backgrounds, often remained absent from school due to inaccessible or unsafe transportation. Recognizing the deep service gap, Bharat Vikas Parishad, in line with Rashtriya Sewa Bharti’s vision of equitable education, began this facility to ensure that disability does not become a barrier to accessing education and essential developmental services.","impact":"The transportation facility has directly improved school attendance among hearing and speech-impaired children. Families now experience relief and reassurance knowing their children travel safely under dedicated care. For many children, this is their first consistent exposure to structured learning. The project has brought a new sense of inclusion and dignity to specially-abled students, giving them the opportunity to develop academically and socially within a supportive environment. It has also created disability awareness in the local community.","achievements":"Since inception, 82 students (61 boys and 21 girls) have benefited from the transportation service. Currently, 36 students are being supported daily. The project has received financial assistance via FCRA channels, ensuring its sustainability. It has succeeded in significantly reducing school dropout among specially-abled children in the region. With a trained team of 16 salaried staff and 75 dedicated volunteers, the centre has earned the trust of parents and schools alike, becoming a model for inclusive service delivery in metropolitan settings.","challenges":"Despite the project’s strong impact, it continues to operate with just one dedicated room, limiting administrative coordination. The transportation needs are growing, but the availability of funds for vehicle maintenance and fuel remains a constraint. Recruiting and retaining sensitized drivers and staff for disability care during transport is also a persistent challenge. Ensuring timely communication with parents and schools in a high-density urban setting requires continuous volunteer engagement and operational precision.","success_story":"Twelve-year-old Ankit, a deaf child from a lower-income family in Seemapuri, had never attended school regularly. His father, a rickshaw puller, and mother, a domestic worker, simply couldn’t afford daily travel expenses or leave work to accompany him. Every morning was a painful decision between work and their son’s future. When Ankit did go to school, he was often late, exhausted, or missed crucial therapy sessions. The result was that he had fallen far behind his classmates.  Everything changed in early 2023 when Ankit was enrolled in the Transportation Facility for Deaf and Dumb Students at Tahirpur. A volunteer visited their home, explained the service, and offered to begin pick-up the very next day. For the first time, Ankit had a vehicle waiting for him outside his home—staffed by people who understood his needs and made him feel safe. His joy knew no bounds. He began arriving at school on time, refreshed and ready to learn. His teachers quickly noticed improvements in his communication, confidence, and attention span.  Within months, Ankit began participating in sign language drama activities and made his first stage appearance at the annual school event. His parents, once burdened with guilt and helplessness, now beam with pride. Ankit is thriving—emotionally, academically, and socially. He dreams of becoming a graphic designer someday.  His story is a testament to how a simple but compassionate service like transportation can become a lifeline of dignity, inclusion, and empowerment for children on the margins. The project not only bridged a logistical gap but also restored hope to a family and illuminated a child’s path toward self-reliance—true to the spirit of She started attending daily reading sessions, patriotic song gatherings, and cultural celebrations, and soon became an active participant in discussion circles. The structured environment and social interactions boosted her confidence, enhanced her mental agility, and instilled a renewed sense of purpose.  Over time, Shanti not only regained her vitality and optimism but also began mentoring other seniors, encouraging them to join the Kendra, participate in activities, and embrace active aging. Her journey reflects the power of inclusive engagement—from loneliness to leadership, from passivity to active contribution.
